coredata.dc:title: Low toxicity of fluorescent carbon quantum dots to white rot fungus Phanerochaete chrysosporium
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qiangqiang
Shi, Rui
Li, Qun
Maimaiti, Tusunniyaze
Lan, Suke
Ouyang, Peng
Ouyang, Bowei
Bai, Yitong
Yu, Baowei
Yang, Sheng-Tao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel fluorescent nanomaterials for applications in diverse areas. With the rapid developments in past decade, the toxicity and environmental safety of CQDs have aroused great interest. In this study, we evaluated the toxicity and impact of CQDs to white rot fungus Phanerochaete chrysosporium, an important fungus for carbon cycle that degrades lignin. Red emissive CQDs slightly stimulated the growth of P. chrysosporium at 50 and 100 μg/mL. CQD aggregates heterogeneously distributed in fungus balls, where the tightly contacted mycelia lost their fibrous structure. Cell wall damage and the detachment of membrane were observed in ultrastructural investigations. CQDs did not affect the laccase and manganese peroxidase activities of P. chrysosporium. The decomposition activity of P. chrysosporium kept unchanged by CQDs according to dye decoloration, sawdust weight loss, and sawdust lignin content. CQDs initiated the protective pathways of P. chrysosporium against oxidative stress, leading to the increases of glutathione and catalase levels and the decreases of malondialdehyde and H2O2 levels. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs in P. chrysosporium implied the biotransformation of CQDs during the one-month incubation. Our results collectively suggested that CQDs were of low toxicity and environmental impacts to white rot fungi.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
White rot fungus
Toxicity
Environmental safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growth and structure of P. Chrysosporium
Influence of CQDs on the fungus growth
Influence of CQDs on mycelium structure and ultrastructure
Impact of CQDs on enzyme activities and decomposition capability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CQDs. (a) TEM image with an inset of higher magnification; (b) C1s XPS spectrum; (c) IR spectrum; (d) Raman spectrum; (e) UV–vis spectrum; (f) FL spectrum.
Absorbance and fluorescence spectra of CQDs. (a) UV–vis spectrum, excitation spectrum (615 nm emission) and emission spectrum (566 nm excitation); (b) FL spectra at different excitation wavelengths.
Weight gains of P. chrysosporium upon the exposure to CQDs. (a) fresh weight; (b) dry weight. * p < 0.05 comparing to the control group.
Structural changes of P. chrysosporium induced by CQDs. (a) control group; (b) 25 μg/mL of CQDs; (c) 100 μg/mL of CQDs; (d) DLS spectrum of CQDs in culture medium after 1 h shaking.

Ultrastructural changes of P. chrysosporium induced by CQDs. (a, d) control group; (b, e) 25 μg/mL of CQDs; (c, f) 100 μg/mL of CQDs. Green arrows pointed to the detachment of cell wall and membrane. Orange arrows indicate the indistinct cell wall.
Influence of CQDs on the enzymatic activity of P. chrysosporium. (a) Lac; (b) MnP. * p < 0.05 comparing to the control group.
Decoloration of reactive brilliant red X-3B by P. chrysosporium in the absence/presence of CQDs.
Biodegradation of sawdust by P. chrysosporium in the absence/presence of CQDs for 3 months. (a) weight loss of sawdust; (b) lignin content of sawdust after biodegradation.
Oxidative stress parameters of P. chrysosporium in the presence/absence of CQDs. (a) CAT; (b) H2O2; (c) GSH; (d) MDA. * p < 0.05 comparing to the control group.

head.ce:title: Low toxicity of fluorescent carbon quantum dots to white rot fungus Phanerochaete chrysosporium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Basic Chemistry of the National Ethnic Affairs Commission, School of Chemistry and Environment, Southwest Minzu University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Basic Chemistry of the National Ethnic Affairs Commission

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Basic Chemistry of the National Ethnic Affairs Commission, School of Chemistry and Environment, Southwest Minzu University, Chengdu 610041, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel fluorescent nanomaterials for applications in diverse areas. With the rapid developments in past decade, the toxicity and environmental safety of CQDs have aroused great interest. In this study, we evaluated the toxicity and impact of CQDs to white rot fungus Phanerochaete chrysosporium, an important fungus for carbon cycle that degrades lignin. Red emissive CQDs slightly stimulated the growth of P. chrysosporium at 50 and 100 μg/mL. CQD aggregates heterogeneously distributed in fungus balls, where the tightly contacted mycelia lost their fibrous structure. Cell wall damage and the detachment of membrane were observed in ultrastructural investigations. CQDs did not affect the laccase and manganese peroxidase activities of P. chrysosporium. The decomposition activity of P. chrysosporium kept unchanged by CQDs according to dye decoloration, sawdust weight loss, and sawdust lignin content. CQDs initiated the protective pathways of P. chrysosporium against oxidative stress, leading to the increases of glutathione and catalase levels and the decreases of malondialdehyde and H2O2 levels. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs in P. chrysosporium implied the biotransformation of CQDs during the one-month incubation. Our results collectively suggested that CQDs were of low toxicity and environmental impacts to white rot fungi.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are fluorescent carbon nanomaterials composed by small carbon nanoparticles (NPs) with surface passivation [1]. Since its discovery in 2006 by Sun and coworkers [2], CQDs have been widely applied in many areas with fast increasing production [3]. CQDs are good fluorescent probes for bioimaging. CQDs are capable to light up cells [4], bacteria [5], and animals [6,7]. CQDs show high performance in converting CO2 into organic substances [8]. CQDs show activities as inhibitors of virus and bacteria [9,10]. The fluorescence sensing of diverse molecules and ions by CQDs has been achieved in environmental and biological analyses [11]. Energy conversion applications of CQDs are also very popular recently with increasing publications each year [12]. Interestingly, CQDs could promote the growth and production of crops [13]. The production of CQDs at kilogram scale has been achieved [14]. Before the commercial applications of CQDs in large-scale, their toxicity and environmental impacts should be thoroughly investigated to ensure the biosafety [15].

Current toxicological evaluations indicate that CQDs are nontoxic or of low toxicity to mammalian cells and animals for bioimaging. In 2009, we reported the first toxicological evaluations of CQDs in vitro and in vivo [16]. CQDs showed similar cytotoxicity to surface passivation reagent polyethylene glycol (PEG). The cytotoxicity of CQDs was regulated by the surface chemistry. Liu et al. compared the cytotoxicity of 2,2′-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) (EDA) and 3-ethoxypropylamine (EPA) functionalized CQDs [17]. EDA&EPA co-functionalized CQDs were more toxic than EDA-CQDs and EPA-CQDs. Havrdova et al. investigated the effect of surface functionalization on the toxicity of CQDs [18]. Polyethylenimine (PEI)-CQDs induced more viability loss than PEG-CQDs and pristine CQDs. PEG-CQDs aroused lower reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels than the others. Yan et al. proved that CQDs of more positive charges were more toxic to human umbilical cord derived mesenchymal stem cells [19]. Mechanistically, oxidative stress and autophagy are possible pathways of CQDs. Ronzani et al. identified the localization of CQDs in lysosome, which led to viability loss, oxidative stress, inflammation, and mitochondrial and lysosomal perturbations at 12 μg/mL [20]. Zhang et al. evidenced that N-doped CQDs located in lysosome and triggered the induction of ROS-mediated cytoprotective autophagy in Hepa1-6 cells at 100 μg/mL and higher [21]. For in vivo toxicity, we reported that PEGylated CQDs were nontoxic to mice [16]. Similar nontoxic nature of CQDs were also reported by other researchers [22,23]. Zheng et al. reported the median lethal dose LD50 of CQDs as 391.615 mg/kg and 357.771 mg/kg for female and male mice [24]. Although CQDs induced temporary toxicity in the repeated dose toxicity evaluation, the parameters became normal after 90 d recovery. Hong et al. did not observe the in vivo toxicity of CQDs to mice, but using 1H NMR-based metabolic approach the trigger of the immune system and the disturbance of cell membrane as well as the normal liver clearance functions were observed [25]. These results largely ensured the safe applications for bioimaging.

The environmental toxicity of CQDs are now attracting more interest along with the applications of CQDs beyond bioimaging. Xiao et al. reported the growth inhibition of microalgae Chlorella pyrenoidosa by CQDs and N-doped CQDs (500 mg/L), and N, S-doped CQDs showed toxicity at lower concentration of 50 mg/L [26]. In contrast, CdTe quantum dots (QDs) showed toxicity at 0.01 mg/mL. Li et al. found that CQDs were nontoxic to Citrus maxima at 600 mg/L, and only slight root activity decrease and oxidative stress were observed at 900 mg/L [27]. Chen et al. observed the toxicity of CQDs to maize (Zea mays L.) at 250 mg/L and higher and attributed the mechanism to oxidative damage [28]. Yao et al. evaluated the effects of CQDs on aquatic species zebrafish Danio rerio, zooplankton Daphnia magna, and phytoplankton Scenedesmus obliquus [29]. CQDs had 96-h EC50 (concentration for 50 % of maximal effect) value of 74.8 mg/L for S. obliquus and 48-h EC50 of 97.5 mg/L for D. magna, while CQDs were nontoxic to D. rerio at 200 mg/L. Sun et al. reported the survival rate decrease of Physa acuta by CQDs at 1 mg/mL [30]. Xiao et al. found that high concentrations of CQDs showed significant development toxicity to embryos and larvae of rare minnow Gobiocypris rarus through oxidative stress and DNA damage [31]. Although the environmental toxicity of CQDs has been concerned, very few studies were performed to investigate the impact of CQDs to microorganisms [32,33].

White rot fungi are crucial microorganisms for the decomposition in carbon cycle, because white rot fungi degrade the lignin containing biomass, e.g. wood and straw [34]. Although CQDs are of low quantity in the environment currently, the production of CQDs would increase along with the success of CQDs in biological applications, which would lead to more CQD release into the environment. When CQDs enter the environment, CQDs would inevitably interact with white rot fungi, thus hold potential threat to the decomposition in carbon cycle. Herein, we evaluated the toxicity and impact of CQDs to white rot fungi Phanerochaete chrysosporium. The weight gains and pH values of P. chrysosporium were recorded after the incubation with CQDs. The mycelia of P. chrysosporium were observed under microscopy upon periodic acid schiff (PAS) staining. The ultrastructure of P. chrysosporium was investigated under transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The enzymatic activities of P. chrysosporium were assayed. The decoloration of reactive brilliant red X-3B, the degradation of sawdust and the change of lignin content of sawdust were measured to reflect the decomposition activity of P. chrysosporium. The oxidative stress parameters were assayed by commercial kits. The fluorescence of CQDs before and after the incubation was recorded by fluorescence imaging facility. The implication to the environmental risk evaluation and safe applications of CQDs is discussed.

CQDs were prepared following the literature protocol with modifications [35]. Briefly, 2.5 g of citric acid and 4 mL of ethylenediamine were added to 160 mL of formamide. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane, and 400 mL of acetone was added to the filtrate. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to collect the precipitate, which was washed with a mixture of 200 mL ethanol and 50 mL acetone. The residue was dried at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain CQDs. CQDs were characterized by TEM (Tecnai G2 20, FEI, USA), infrared spectrum (IR, Magna-IR 750, Nicolet, USA), Raman spectra (inVia, Renishaw, UK), X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo-Fisher, USA), dynamic light scattering (DLS, Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK), UV–vis spectroscopy (UV-1800, Mapada, Shanghai, China) and fluorescence spectroscopy (FL, Dual-FL, Horiba, Japan) before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth and structure of P. Chrysosporium
ce:section.ce:para: The fungal strain P. chrysosporium (MTCC 787) was purchased from the Guangdong Microbiology Culture Center, Guangzhou, China. The culture protocol of P. chrysosporium was following our previous reports [36,37]. CQDs were added to the culture medium at designed concentrations (0−100 μg/mL) and the pH values were adjusted to 5.0 (PB10, Sartorius Co., Germany). The fungus spores were inoculated to 40 mL of culture medium at 5 × 105 spore/mL. The fungi were incubated at 37 °C and 100 rpm for 7–28 d. At designed time points, fungus balls were filtered with filter paper. The pH values of the filtrates were measured. After removing the apparent water, the fresh weights of fungus balls were measured. The fungus balls were dried at 60 °C for 48 h for dry weight measurements.

The fungus balls were also subjected to mycelium structure and cellular structure observations. For mycelium structure observation, the fungus balls were fixed with 4 % formaldehyde and processed following standard protocol for PAS staining [38]. The PAS images were photographed on a microscope equipped with charge coupled device (CAB-30PC, Carbontek Co., Chengdu, China). For cellular structure observation, the fungus balls were fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde and processed following standard protocol for ultrathin section and poststain [38]. The copper grids containing ultrathin sections were checked under TEM.

In addition, the fungus balls with/without CQD exposure were imaged on a PerkinElmer (USA) IVIS® Lumina III in vivo imaging system. Twelve excitation/emission wavelength pairs (460 nm/520 nm, 460 nm/570 nm, 460 nm/620 nm, 480 nm/520 nm, 480 nm/570 nm, 480 nm/620 nm, 500 nm/570 nm, 500 nm/620 nm, 520 nm/570 nm, 520 nm/620 nm, 540 nm/620 nm, and 560 nm/620 nm) were used for the imaging to achieve the spectral un-mixing automatically by the Living Image® software (PerkinElmer Inc., USA). The fungus ball without CQDs was chosen as the reference signal of autofluorescence and a syringe containing CQDs was simultaneously imaged as the reference of real fluorescence signal. A fungus ball directly injected with CQDs was imaged, too. Radiant efficiency was adopted for the fluorescence intensity in the color-coded images.

P. chrysosporium was inoculated at the density of 5 × 105 spore/mL and exposed to 40 mL of CQDs containing culture medium at 0−100 μg/mL. P. chrysosporium was incubated at 37 °C and 100 rpm for 7, 14 and 28 d. At designed time points, the fermentation broth was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant for laccase (Lac) and manganese peroxidase (MnP) activity measurements. Lac activity was analyzed by 2, 2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic (ABTS) method [36,37], and MnP activity was measured by the MnSO4 method [37].

The decomposition activity of P. chrysosporium was first measured by the decoloration of reactive brilliant red X-3B. The fungus spores were inoculated at the density of 5 × 105 spore/mL and then exposed to CQDs at 0−100 μg/mL. The flasks were shaken at 37 °C and 100 rpm. At Day 3, reactive brilliant red X-3B was introduced to the flasks at a final concentration of 30 mg/L. The flasks were incubated for another 4 d and the supernatant absorbance@538 nm was recorded on UV–vis spectrometer (UV1600, Shanghai Mapada Instruments Co., China). The C/C 0 was calculated by A/A 0, where A was the absorbance at designed time point and A 0 was the initial absorbance.

Separately, the decomposition activity of P. chrysosporium was indicated by the sawdust degradation. Sawdust was sterilized and dried before use. To each flask, 15 mL of solid fermentation medium, 5 g of sawdust and CQDs of different final concentrations (0−100 μg/mL) were added. The flasks were inoculated with 1 × 107 spores/flask and statically incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 3 months. At the end of degradation, the sawdust samples were thoroughly washed with deionized water and dried to constant weights at 80 °C. The percentage of weight loss was calculated from the dry weight of sawdust before and after degradation. The contents of lignin were measured following strictly following the kit instructions (Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd, Beijing, China, http://www.solarbio.com/goods-64477.html).

Oxidative stress parameters, including H2O2, catalase (CAT), glutathione (GSH) and malondialdehyde (MDA), were measured after the exposure of P. chrysosporium to CQDs. The culture medium was supplemented with CQDs at 0−100 μg/mL and inoculated at the density of 5 × 105 spore/mL. The flasks were incubated at 37 °C and 100 rpm for 14 d. The fungus balls were filtered, homogenized and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatants were collected for protein concentration and oxidative stress parameter measurements strictly following the kit instructions (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, Nanjing, China, http://www.njjcbio.com/).

The data were expressed as the mean of individual observations with standard deviation (mean ± SD). Significance was calculated using Student's t-test, where p < 0.05 was considered as the statistical significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CQDs on the fungus growth
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs did not affect the fresh weights of P. chrysosporium (p > 0.05, Fig. 3 a). No apparent inhibition was observed at all exposed concentrations at 7, 14 and 28 d. After drying, the fungus dry weights showed significant increases after exposures to CQDs at 7 and 14 d (Fig. 3b). After 7 d, the dry weights of P. chrysosporium were 135 % of control at 50 μg/mL and 212 % of control at 100 μg/mL. The values of 14 d were 177 % of control at 50 μg/mL and 216 % of control at 100 μg/mL. The stimulation of dry weights disappeared at 28 d. CQDs did not affect the pH values of culture medium (Fig. S1). The biomass measurements indicated that CQDs was nontoxic to fungus growth with stimulating effect. Only the dry weight was stimulated implied that CQDs might affect the water content of fungus balls. Nanomaterials would affect the water content of biological tissues, such as moss (by GO) [38], pea (by reduced GO, RGO) [39] and white rot fungi (by carbon nanotubes, CNTs) [37]. According to the literature, CQDs were among the most biocompatible nanomaterials to white rot fungi. Water dispersible carboxylated fullerene and graphene oxide (GO) inhibited the dry weight of P. chrysosporium seriously [40,41]. Water dispersible nanodiamonds (NDs) showed similar effect on the growth of P. chrysosporium to stimulate the growth at 50 μg/mL and higher [42]. For non-dispersible carbon nanomaterials, the lack of contact between carbon nanomaterials and P. chrysosporium resulted in ignorable impact on fungus growth, e.g. RGO [36], CNTs [37] and pristine fullerene [41]. Separately, metal containing nanomaterials were generally toxic to white rot fungi, such as Ag NPs [43], Au NPs [44], Fe2O3 NPs [45] and CdSe QDs [46]. Overall, the low toxicity of CQDs could be due to the lack of toxic components, the aggregation during incubation and the lack of sharp edges/ends.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CQDs on mycelium structure and ultrastructure
ce:section.ce:para: P. chrysosporium has obvious mycelia in the fungus balls. Upon PAS staining, the fibrous structure of mycelia was presented under microscope in the control group (Fig. 4 a). Despite the majorly of fibers, very a few of short rots were also observed. At low concentration of 25 μg/mL, the mycelia were unchanged in most areas, but some of them lost the fibrous structure. The phenomenon became more distinguishable at 100 μg/mL. In Fig. 3c, there were distinct areas of fibrous mycelia (orange square) and disturbed amorphous structure (green square). By enlarging the representative areas, dark aggregates were observed in the amorphous area (green square) and few aggregates were found in the fibrous area (orange square). The PAS staining results indicated that CQDs aggregated in P. chrysosporium fungus balls to disturb the mycelium structure. The aggregation in fungus balls was more serious than in culture medium. According to DLS, the main aggregation peak was at 220 nm (Fig. 4d), while the diameters of aggregates were several micrometers. The aggregations could be due to two possibilities, namely the oxidation of CQDs and the interaction between CQDs and the secretions of fungi. Similar phenomenon was observed in the evaluation of NDs [42]. The aggregates of NDs damaged the directly contacted mycelia. Together with the results in the current study, we concluded that CQDs aggregated during the interaction with mycelia and the CQD aggregates induced structural changes to mycelia. On the other hand, the aggregation decreased the interaction of CQDs with mycelia, restricted the interaction in limited area, thus resulting in low toxicity. As discussed above, other carbon nanomaterial aggregates also were of low toxicity to P. chrysosporium, such as RGO [36], CNTs [37] and pristine fullerene [41].

TEM observations were undertaken to reveal the ultrastructural changes of fungus cells. The small round cells showed intact cell wall in the control group with a few of them rod-like (Fig. 5 a&d). At 25 μg/mL of CQDs, the cell wall damage and cell wall-membrane detachment were observed occasionally (Fig. 5b&e). At 100 μg/mL of CQDs, the indistinct cell wall was observed, too (Fig. 5c&f). No other abnormal characteristics were found under TEM. In addition, no CQDs were observed in the fungus cells, suggesting that CQDs did not across the cell wall and membrane. The lack of cellular uptake in fungus cells was quite different to the results of mammalian cells [47–49]. The tight cell wall of fungus cells hindered the penetration of CQDs into cells. The mammalian cells had soft cell membrane only, which could take CQDs via endocytosis [2,47–49]. The lack of cellular uptake of CQDs could partially explained the low toxicity of CQDs. CQDs did not contain toxic elements to release in the culture medium, so the only possible impact was the physical contact, which might induce mechanical damage during the shaking incubation. Such mechanical damages were observed in the studies of graphene and CNTs, which had higher mechanical strength and sharper edges/ends. For example, Pulingam reported that inactivation of Gram-negative bacteria by GO mainly occurred through physical contact which led to membrane damage [50]. Jiang et al. found that the mechanical penetration of A549 cells led to the cytotoxicity of CNTs [51]. Beyond the cellular studies, we observed the toxicity of CNTs in mice [52], while CQDs were nontoxic after intravenous injection under very similar situations [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of CQDs on enzyme activities and decomposition capability
ce:section.ce:para: Secretions of Lac, MnP and ligninase are the well accepted mechanism for the degradation of lignin by white rot fungi. In our specific cultivation system, P. chrysosporium mainly produced Lac and MnP, but did not secret ligninase [36,42]. CQDs did not affect the Lac activities at 7 and 14 d (Fig. 6 a). At 28 d, the high concentrations of CQDs (10−100 μg/mL) inhibited the Lac activities, but the difference was insignificant. The inhibition of MnP (70.7 % of control) was significant at 28 d and 100 μg/mL of CQDs (Fig. 6b). Overall, CQDs had very limited impact on the enzymatic activities.

The oxidative decomposition activity of P. chrysosporium might lead to the transformation of CQDs. However, we failed to separate CQDs from fungus balls after incubation. Instead, we imaged CQDs containing fungus balls on a fluorescence imaging facility (Fig. S2). No fluorescence was observed in CQDs containing fungus balls after incubation and fungus balls without CQDs exposure. Direct injection of CQDs into fungus balls could light up the fungus balls, suggesting that fungus mycelia did not affect the fluorescence of CQDs. Although there were several reports indicating the complete oxidation of CQDs into CO2 in biological systems [53], the dark aggregates in fungus balls suggested that CQDs were not converted into CO2 by P. chrysosporium. Thus, the quenching of fluorescence after incubation was due to the transformation of CQDs, where the oxidation of CQDs changed the surface functionalities and broke the surface energy traps [1,2]. The oxidation of carbon nanomaterials was previously reported in the literature. We observed the increase of oxygen content in RGO after the transformation by P. chrysosporium [54]. P. chrysosporium could shorten CNTs during the 30-d incubation [55]. The degradation of CQDs into CO2 was reported by Li et al. [53]. In future, new separation and systematic characterization of CQDs in white rot fungi should be performed to identify the transformation of CQDs and the underneath mechanism.

The unaffected enzyme activity and decomposition capability of P. chrysosporium could be regarded as an advantage of CQDs over other nanomaterials. According to the literature, the decomposition activity loss depended on the species and properties of nanomaterials. Carboxylated fullerene completely inhibited the Lac and MnP activities as well as the decoloration of reactive brilliant red X-3B, while pristine fullerene did not [37]. Carboxylated fullerene largely inhibited the degradation of sawdust. Similarly, dispersive GO completely inhibited the decoloration of reactive brilliant red X-3B, but RGO did not [36,42,54]. The situation was more complicated for CNTs, where pristine CNTs inhibited the degradation of sawdust more than oxidized CNTs [37]. That could be explained by the cellular uptake of pristine CNTs. In addition, NDs inhibited the enzymatic activities and decoloration of reactive brilliant red X-3B at high concentrations, but the sawdust degradation was unchanged [42]. The degradation of wood by white rot fungi could be alleviated by TiO2 NPs [56]. Ag NPs and Cu NPs significantly improved resistance of particleboard to T. versicolor [57]. Beyond the inhibition effects, Fe2O3 NPs were found to facilitate the bisphenol A degradation by white rot fungi [58]. The decomposition activity is crucial for the ecological function and environmental applications of P. chrysosporium. The degradation of lignin in straw and wood helps the organic carbon to return into atmosphere as CO2 [34]. The environmental remediation of organic pollutants is effective through the oxidation by P. chrysosporium [34]. Therefore, the applications of CQDs were safer than other nanomaterials for white rot fungi in the carbon cycle.

In previous studies of P. chrysosporium, oxidative stress is well acknowledged as the toxicological mechanism [59]. Herein, we measured the related parameters of P. chrysosporium to identify the oxidative stress after the exposure to CQDs (Fig. 9 ). CAT levels increased at CQD concentrations of 10−100 μg/mL, and the increase was statistically significant at 50 μg/mL. However, the decreases of H2O2 levels were insignificant. Another group of parameters showed the same trend. The GSH levels insignificantly increased along with the increases of CQD concentrations. Correspondingly, the MDA levels decreased simultaneously. The MDA levels largely decreased at 10 μg/mL and higher. These data suggested that CQDs initiated the pathways against oxidative damage to protect the fungus cells. This phenomenon was distinct from other nanomaterials. NDs increased the MDA level of P. chrysosporium [42]. CdSe/ZnS QDs induced the meaningful oxidative damage to P. chrysosporium [46]. On the other hand, the microorganism species also affected the oxidative stress. Bagheri et al. assessed the toxicity of QDs to yeast cells Pichia pastoris [32]. Cell growth was inhibited at 10 mg/mL and higher, and the mitochondrial activity decreases were observed at 5 mg/mL. Light irradiation enhanced the ROS generation, leading to more growth inhibition. Xiao et al. attributed the toxicity of microalgae C. pyrenoidosa by CQDs, N-doped CQDs and N, S-doped CQDs to oxidative damage as reflected by GSH decreases and MDA increases [26]. The oxidative stress aroused by CQDs was also reported in cells, animals and plants, too [21,28,31].

In summary, CQDs showed very low toxicity to white rot fungi, and did not affect their decomposition activity. CQDs stimulated the growth of P. chrysosporium at 50 and 100 μg/mL. The aggregation of CQDs alleviated the direct contact between CQDs and fungus mycelia, where only the direct contacted mycelia lost the fibrous structure. CQDs did not affect the Lac and MnP activities, thus the decomposition activity of P. chrysosporium kept unchanged after the exposure to CQDs. No oxidative damage was induced by CQDs to P. chrysosporium. It is hoped that our results would stimulate more interest on the environmental effects of carbon nanomaterials and benefit the safe applications of carbon nanomaterials.

Qiangqiang Zhang: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Rui Shi: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Qun Li: Investigation, Methodology. Tusunniyaze Maimaiti: Visualization. Suke Lan: Formal analysis. Peng Ouyang: Investigation. Bowei Ouyang: Methodology. Yitong Bai: Resources. Baowei Yu: Writing - review & editing. Sheng-Tao Yang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the China Natural Science Foundation (No. 21777132), Top-notch Young Talents Program of China, and Innovation Scientific Research Program for Graduates of Southwest Minzu University (CX2019SZ56).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jece.2020.104633.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functionalized carbon nanoparticles: syntheses and applications in optical bioimaging and energy conversion
Coord. Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.E. LeCroy, S.T. Yang, F. Yang, Y. Liu, K.A.S. Fernando, C.E. Bunker, Y. Hu, P.G. Luo, Y.P. Sun, Functionalized carbon nanoparticles: syntheses and applications in optical bioimaging and energy conversion, Coord. Chem. Rev. 320 (2016) 66-81.
